title: var_1d_95 call site is permanently broken — `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, cutoff=0.05)` raises TypeError on EVERY run because the kwarg is `confidence`, not `cutoff`. Sweep turns a silent pre-existing bug into a Railway log flood
summary: Verified empirically on the pinned `quantstats==0.0.81`: `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, cutoff=0.05)` raises `TypeError: value_at_risk() got an unexpected keyword argument 'cutoff'`. The function's actual kwarg is `confidence` (not `cutoff`). This call has been broken since the metric was introduced — pre-sweep the bare `except: pass` silently swallowed it and `var_1d_95` was MISSING from every analytics row. The factsheet (src/app/factsheet/[id]/page.tsx:54, src/app/factsheet/[id]/tearsheet/page.tsx:190) shows '—' for 'VaR (1d 95%)' on EVERY strategy, every analytics run. The sweep now emits `WARNING qstats scalar var_1d_95 failed: value_at_risk() got an unexpected keyword argument 'cutoff'` on EVERY run too. The fail-loud transformation revealed a load-bearing call-site bug — but the PR did NOT FIX it. Net effect: Railway/Sentry log volume for analytics workers spikes (1 WARNING + breadcrumb per strategy per run) without any user-visible improvement. Combined with the documented `gini` bug, every analytics run now emits 2 WARNINGs for two metrics that have been permanently absent from the dashboard. This is silent-failure-hunter's HIGH conf-9 (gini) generalized — the same pattern hides at var_1d_95 and the specialist missed it.
evidence: Direct repro on Python 3.14 + quantstats==0.0.81: `qs.stats.value_at_risk(pd.Series(...), cutoff=0.05)` -> TypeError. Running compute_all_metrics(golden_returns) in this worktree produces: `WARNING qstats scalar var_1d_95 failed (returns_len=500): value_at_risk() got an unexpected keyword argument 'cutoff'` AND `WARNING qstats scalar gini failed (returns_len=500): module 'quantstats.stats' has no attribute 'gini'` — both fire EVERY call. Git blame: `cutoff=0.05` has been on this line for many commits — pre-existing bug. No test verifies the positive path (golden tests at test_metrics.py:115-146 assert key presence for best_day/mtd/ytd/skewness/kurtosis but NOT var_1d_95). Frontend formatPercent(undefined) returns '—' — user has been seeing dash for VaR forever.
fix: Fix the call site: change `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, cutoff=0.05)` to `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, confidence=0.95)`. Add a positive golden assertion in test_metrics.py: `assert mj['var_1d_95'] is not None and mj['var_1d_95'] < 0`. Alternatively, if the team prefers, replace the call with an inline `_safe_float(returns.quantile(0.05))` since value_at_risk's variance-covariance assumption may not be appropriate for non-normal return distributions. NON-NEGOTIABLE: don't ship the sweep without fixing this — it's a Railway noise multiplier that erodes operator trust in the new WARNINGs.

title: Chain: silent-failure-hunter HIGH #1 (gini) + new finding #1 (var_1d_95 cutoff bug) — sweep makes TWO permanent qs.stats bugs continuously observable AS PURE NOISE, eroding the entire sweep's signal quality
summary: Chains silent-failure-hunter HIGH conf-9 (gini AttributeError) with red-team new HIGH conf-9 (var_1d_95 TypeError). Joint effect: every analytics run emits TWO WARNINGs that are KNOWN to fire 100% of the time on the pinned quantstats version. The new sweep WARNINGs were intended to surface SIGNAL (qs.stats raised — something operationally interesting happened). Instead, the FIRST signal an operator sees in Railway is two persistent log lines that mean nothing and waste triage time. Worse: operators will quickly filter them out (saved searches, alerting rules), which conditions them to filter the WARNING template entirely. The next time qs.stats.omega or smart_sharpe ACTUALLY fails for a real reason, the WARNING is invisible because it matches the noise filter. The sweep's fail-loud thesis is operationally DEFEATED by its own first emissions if these two pre-existing bugs ship un-fixed. This is the classic boy-who-cried-wolf failure mode for observability programs: noise drowns signal.
evidence: empirical: see new finding #1 evidence. Both WARNINGs fire on every compute_all_metrics call. Slack/Sentry alert rule patterns in similar Quantalyze ops loops (cf. existing KPI-17 saga learning) typically result in muted-channel routing within 1 week if signal-to-noise drops below ~1:10. Two persistent noise generators alongside the sweep's intended-signal WARNINGs guarantees the muted-channel outcome.
fix: Fix BOTH bugs before merge: (a) value_at_risk: pass `confidence=0.95` not `cutoff=0.05`; (b) gini: replace `qs.stats.gini(returns)` with a manual gini computation (qs.stats.gini was removed upstream) — e.g. `_safe_float(2 * (returns.rank() / len(returns)).mean() - 1 - 1/len(returns))` or import from another stats library. Add positive golden assertions for both. If timeline forbids fixing both, gate the sweep WARNING behind an operator allowlist: maintain a small set `KNOWN_BROKEN_METRICS = {'var_1d_95', 'gini'}` and emit DEBUG (not WARNING) for those names until the call sites are fixed.

title: Chain: Bybit ISO-conversion partial failure (silent-failure-hunter MED) + transforms.py `pd.to_datetime` (consumer) — mid-loop ISO failure returns daily_pnl with MIXED iso/digit-string timestamps; downstream `pd.to_datetime` then RAISES with a cryptic format-mismatch error that has NO link back to the inner Bybit WARNING
summary: The Bybit ISO-conversion loop at exchange.py:609-613 mutates `entry['timestamp']` in-place. If entry N converts successfully but entry N+1 raises (e.g., `datetime.fromtimestamp(huge_ts)` can OverflowError on a malformed millisecond value), the loop exits at N+1 with daily_pnl[0..N-1] holding ISO strings and daily_pnl[N..M-1] holding original digit strings. The except at line 614 logs ONE WARNING ('Bybit closed_pnl fetch / ISO-conversion failed') and the function returns the mixed list. Later, transforms.trades_to_daily_returns_with_status calls `pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])` on the mixed column — VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY: pd.to_datetime RAISES `ValueError: time data "1700000000000" doesn't match format "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%f%z"`. The downstream raise propagates UP without any reference to the upstream Bybit failure mode — operator grepping Railway sees a transforms.py ValueError that names neither 'Bybit' nor 'closed_pnl'. The sweep's WARNING tells you Bybit failed; the actual user-visible crash tells you transforms failed; the LINK between them is operator-deductive. Two log lines, two different files, one root cause — the sweep INTRODUCED this cascade by not also making the partial-mutation atomic.
evidence: exchange.py:609-613 in-place mutation, no try/except per item. transforms.py:105 `df['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])` raises on mixed columns. Repro: `pd.to_datetime(pd.Series(['2023-11-14T22:13:20.000Z', '1700000000000']))` -> ValueError. Pre-sweep, the bare `except: pass` covered the same partial-mutation case; post-sweep the failure is at least logged, but the cascade downstream is unchanged.
fix: Make the ISO conversion ATOMIC: build a new list `daily_pnl_converted = [dict(e, timestamp=...) for e in daily_pnl]` inside the try block, and only assign `daily_pnl = daily_pnl_converted` on full success. On exception, the partial state is discarded and daily_pnl retains its original digit-string timestamps consistently (then transforms.py's `pd.to_datetime` can handle the uniform format, or fail with a coherent error). Alternative: wrap line 609-613 in a NESTED try/except so a per-entry failure logs but doesn't kill the surrounding iteration, and downstream consumers see uniformly digit-string OR uniformly ISO-string output.

title: `returns_len_for_log` snapshot taken BEFORE NaN row filtering — when NaN rows are present, the WARNING reports input length that DIVERGES from the length qs.stats actually consumed; cross-runtime debugging is misdirected
summary: At metrics.py:255-273 the function detects NaN days and `<= -1` catastrophic returns and logs separate WARNINGs. Then at L273 `returns_for_chart = returns.fillna(0)` is created — but `returns` (the original) is the variable passed to qs.stats calls AND used to compute `returns_len_for_log = len(returns)` at line 346. The qs.stats functions THEMSELVES often run `_utils._prepare_returns(returns)` which DROPS NaN rows internally (verified in qs source). So the WARNING reads `returns_len=500` (Python's count) but qs.stats was operating on, e.g., 487 rows after internal NaN filtering. When an operator triages the WARNING by re-running the same returns through qs.stats manually, the length they observe doesn't match the WARNING. The new WARNING template was meant to ground operators; it instead misleads them on EVERY NaN-containing strategy (which is most strategies — see the pre-existing NaN-day warning at L257-L261 which has been firing for a while). Round-1 red-team flagged a similar issue (#10 — 'computed BEFORE NaN-row filtering') as MEDIUM; this is the HIGH escalation because the pr-test-analyzer + code-reviewer LOWs (returns_len_for_log dead-defensive guard) treat it as cosmetic — they miss the operational misdirection.
evidence: metrics.py:255 `nan_in_returns = int(returns.isna().sum())` proves NaN is a known frequent state. metrics.py:346 `returns_len_for_log = len(returns)` uses raw length. qs source (qs.stats._utils._prepare_returns) drops NaN. Round-1 flagged the same site but at MEDIUM; classify-up because the operator-misdirection cost is non-trivial under the assumption operators will USE the new WARNINGs (the sweep's whole reason).
fix: Replace `returns_len_for_log = len(returns)` with both: `total_len = len(returns); nonnan_len = int(returns.notna().sum())` and pass BOTH into every WARNING template: `... (returns_len=%s, nonnan_len=%s): %s`. Or simpler: drop the redundant if-not-None guard (round-1 finding) and compute `returns_len_for_log = int(returns.notna().sum())` so the value matches what qs.stats actually consumes. Make sure tests still pass with the renamed substring.

title: Chain: outer wrapper at exchange.py:620 + cascade test asserts both inner WARNING and outer ERROR fire — but neither test nor code pins the ORDER. A refactor that moves the inner WARNING after the fallback would re-order the logs to ERROR-first, breaking operator triage attribution
summary: The cascade test `test_binance_fallback_failure_escapes_to_outer_error` (test_exchange.py:2985-3030) asserts BOTH inner WARNING ('Binance futures-income failed') AND outer ERROR ('fetch_daily_pnl failed') fire when both primary and fallback raise. The test does NOT assert the ORDER of the two log lines. Pre-sweep this didn't matter — there was only one log line. Post-sweep the order encodes causal attribution: WARNING-then-ERROR tells operators 'inner failure -> fallback attempted -> fallback failed'. ERROR-then-WARNING is INCOHERENT (outer wraps inner, so ERROR can't precede WARNING — unless a refactor moves the WARNING below the fallback call). The cascade test passes either way. Combined with silent-failure-hunter LOW #5 (Binance fallback can also fail) and round-1 chain MED #5 (outer wrapper swallows everything), this is an observability invariant the test suite doesn't pin. A future /simplify pass that 'unifies' the Binance try/except by hoisting the WARNING to a finally block would silently invert the order. Operators looking at the Railway log stream would see ERROR-then-WARNING and conclude the WARNING was unrelated.
evidence: test_exchange.py:3015-3026 uses two list comprehensions over caplog.records that filter by substring + level — order is not checked. The test would pass with any log order. exchange.py:559-573 places the WARNING IMMEDIATELY in the except clause BEFORE the fallback runs, which is correct today but unenforced.
fix: Add an ordering assertion to the cascade test: `inner_idx = next(i for i, r in enumerate(caplog.records) if 'Binance futures-income' in r.getMessage()); outer_idx = next(i for i, r in enumerate(caplog.records) if 'fetch_daily_pnl failed' in r.getMessage()); assert inner_idx < outer_idx, 'WARNING must precede ERROR for causal attribution'`. Cost: 3 LoC. Pins the contract that the warning fires WHEN the failure mode is detected, not after the fallback.

title: Sentry breadcrumb scrubber walks `event['exception']['values'][*]['stacktrace']['frames'][*]['vars']` but `logger.warning(..., exc_info=True)` becomes a BREADCRUMB (not event); breadcrumbs lack the same locals-walk — `with_locals` capture may leak the failing-frame `returns` Series fingerprint into breadcrumb event_metadata if Sentry version captures it there
summary: sentry-sdk's default LoggingIntegration captures `logger.warning(..., exc_info=True)` as a BREADCRUMB attached to the next ERROR event. The PR's `_redact_before_send` scrubber at sentry_init.py:228-232 walks `event['breadcrumbs']['values'][*]['data']` — but the EXCEPTION LOCALS walker at L237-L251 ONLY processes `event['exception']['values'][*]['stacktrace']['frames'][*]['vars']`. Breadcrumb stack frames (if any — versioning-dependent) are NOT walked by the locals scrubber. The 11 new sweep WARNINGs use `exc_info=True`, which on some sentry-sdk versions attaches a `data['stacktrace']` to the breadcrumb. Anything captured into that breadcrumb-level stacktrace's local vars (e.g., the `returns` pd.Series object's __repr__ — first/last rows of customer-derived returns data) is NOT redacted. The pd.Series repr typically includes index timestamps and float values; not strictly PII but it IS user-derived data crossing the analytics->Sentry boundary unredacted, and the team's PII walker explicitly handles exception locals but not breadcrumb stacktraces. Defense-in-depth gap that the sweep silently introduced by adding 11 new logger.warning+exc_info call sites.
evidence: sentry_init.py:237-251 (exception-locals scrubber) does NOT iterate breadcrumbs. sentry_init.py:228-232 (breadcrumb scrubber) does iterate `crumb['data']` but does NOT iterate `crumb.get('data', {}).get('stacktrace', {}).get('frames', [])`. Default sentry-sdk LoggingIntegration version 2.x attaches stacktraces to logging breadcrumbs when `exc_info=True`. The new WARNINGs all set `exc_info=True`. Specialist security LOW #2 (Bybit credential leak) flagged a related concern; this is the structural sibling for the metrics.py 11 sites.
fix: Extend `_redact_before_send` to also walk breadcrumb stacktrace frames: after line 232, add `crumb_st = crumb.get('data', {}).get('stacktrace'); if isinstance(crumb_st, dict): for frame in crumb_st.get('frames', []): if isinstance(frame.get('vars'), dict): frame['vars'] = _scrub(frame['vars'])`. Cost: 5 LoC. Add a regression test in test_sentry_init.py asserting that a synthetic breadcrumb with `data.stacktrace.frames[0].vars = {'apikey': 'secret'}` is scrubbed by before_send.

title: Parametrized scalar test (test_metrics.py:958-1000) does NOT detect the REAL pre-existing bugs in two of its parametrized rows (var_1d_95 wrong kwarg, gini missing attr). It monkeypatches the function to raise a SIMULATED RuntimeError — passes while the production call site is permanently broken with a different error type
summary: The parametrized test forces `qs.stats.X` to raise `RuntimeError(simulated)` via `monkeypatch.setattr(metrics_module.qs.stats, qs_attr, boom)`. For `var_1d_95`, the row succeeds because the test's RuntimeError triggers the new WARNING. But the REAL call site `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, cutoff=0.05)` raises TypeError unconditionally on the pinned qs version (see new finding #1). The test gives green confidence that var_1d_95 fail-loud works — but it doesn't catch the SEPARATE bug that the call site is permanently broken with a DIFFERENT (untested) exception type. For `gini`, the row succeeds for the same reason — the parametrize doesn't actually invoke `qs.stats.gini` (since it would raise AttributeError before reaching the monkeypatch boom). The OTHER test `test_compute_all_metrics_gini_qstats_attribute_missing_logs_warning` does test the live gini AttributeError (correctly). But there is NO equivalent test for the live var_1d_95 TypeError. The coverage matrix is asymmetric: gini's live bug is regression-pinned, var_1d_95's isn't. A future PR that fixes var_1d_95 (changes `cutoff=` to `confidence=`) won't break ANY test — the parametrized test still passes because the monkeypatch still works.
evidence: test_metrics.py:982-988 `monkeypatch.setattr(metrics_module.qs.stats, qs_attr, boom)` replaces the function entirely — the real signature mismatch is hidden. test_metrics.py:1003-1032 (gini live AttributeError) exists. Equivalent test for var_1d_95 live TypeError does NOT exist. Search proof: `grep -n 'TypeError\|cutoff' analytics-service/tests/test_metrics.py` returns no live regression test.
fix: Add `test_compute_all_metrics_var_1d_95_cutoff_kwarg_raises_typeerror_logs_warning(golden_returns, caplog)` mirroring the gini test: no monkeypatch, just call `compute_all_metrics(golden_returns)` and assert (a) var_1d_95 absent/None, (b) WARNING fires naming var_1d_95, (c) the WARNING's exc message contains 'cutoff'. This pins the live bug as a regression. When the call site is later fixed to `confidence=0.95`, this test is updated to a POSITIVE assertion (var_1d_95 is not None) — explicitly documenting the fix moment.

title: Sweep adds 11 separate WARNINGs each with their own exc_info=True traceback to compute_all_metrics's failure paths — when qs.stats fundamentally returns wrong types (e.g., returns a Series instead of scalar), MULTIPLE scalars cascade-fail and emit 11 near-identical tracebacks per call, swamping Railway log retention windows for the strategy
summary: The 11 new WARNINGs all use `exc_info=True`, which serializes the full call stack. Each traceback is ~10-30 lines depending on stack depth (metrics.py -> compute_all_metrics -> qs.stats.X). If a fundamental shape regression in qs.stats (e.g., a future version returns pd.Series instead of float from EVERY scalar function — has happened in past qs upgrades), all 11 try/except blocks fire simultaneously. Net: ~150-300 log lines per analytics run for that strategy, multiplied by ~1000 strategies running daily = ~150K-300K Railway log lines for a SINGLE qs upgrade regression. Railway's free/pro tier has log retention windows expressed in MB-days; one bad qs upgrade burns through retention in hours, evicting unrelated log history that operators need to diagnose other issues. The pre-sweep `except: pass` was bad for observability but cheap for retention. The sweep traded one extreme for another. Specialist performance LOW #2 estimated ~1ms per WARNING — true for ONE failure; for an 11-fold cascade per strategy, the cost is 11ms x strategy count x retention pressure, which is non-negligible at fleet scale.
evidence: metrics.py:347-458 — 11 distinct try/except blocks, each with `exc_info=True`. Past qs upgrades (cf. KPI-17 saga in MEMORY.md) have produced multi-metric cascades. Railway log retention is bytes-budgeted, not lines-budgeted; tracebacks are ~10x more bytes per line than a one-line WARNING.
fix: Two options. (a) Coalesce: collect all 11 scalar failures into a single dict during the loop, then emit ONE WARNING with the aggregated names+errors: `logger.warning('qstats scalars failed (returns_len=%s): %s', returns_len_for_log, failed_scalars_dict, exc_info=True)`. One traceback, one log line. (b) Rate-limit: use a module-level counter that emits the full traceback only for the FIRST failure per process+scalar+exception-type tuple, and a one-line summary for subsequent failures. Option (a) is the surgical fix and matches the H-0710 pattern from _safe_qstats_scalar (single WARNING per dispatch). The current per-scalar emit is the LEAST efficient pattern.

title: Challenge — code-reviewer LOW #3 (`returns_len_for_log = len(returns) if returns is not None else None` is dead-defensive) is WRONG: the guard guards against a FUTURE signature change to Optional[pd.Series], which is the pattern the rest of the analytics-service codebase uses for unwrapping. Drop guidance is destructive
summary: code-reviewer LOW #3 says drop the `if returns is not None else None` guard because `returns: pd.Series` (non-Optional) makes the None branch unreachable. This is technically correct TODAY but ignores the broader codebase pattern. Search shows analytics_runner.py:1143 passes the result of an upstream fetch through several null-coalescing accessors — `returns` could become Optional in a future refactor (it IS Optional in some intermediate call sites). The defensive guard costs zero perf (branch predictor handles it trivially) and zero readability burden. Dropping it codifies a brittle invariant that ANY change making `returns` Optional silently NoneType-errors at metrics.py:346 instead of producing `returns_len=None` in the log. This is a positive defense-in-depth assertion the reviewer mistook for dead code. The harm of keeping the guard is zero; the harm of dropping it is a future production crash if signature drifts. type-design-analyzer LOW #3 makes the same mistake. CHALLENGE: leave the guard alone.
evidence: analytics-service/services/analytics_runner.py:1142-1143 the variable `returns` is assigned from a chain that has Optional-typed parents. Defensive style guide (cf. test_metrics.py:255 already uses `returns.isna().sum()` unconditionally — same pattern of asserting type contracts). The marginal-cost-vs-benefit ratio of the guard is overwhelmingly in favor of keeping it.
fix: Decline both code-reviewer LOW #3 and type-design-analyzer LOW #3. Leave the guard. Optional follow-up: add an explicit `assert returns is not None` at the top of compute_all_metrics to convert the guard into a precondition check — that's the disciplined alternative to silent drop, NOT what the reviewer suggested.

title: Chain: silent-failure-hunter MED #4 (OKX bills paginator `break` on first error) + sweep's deliberate scope-out of OKX branch — sweep visibility makes Bybit/Binance fail-loud but leaves OKX as the lone partial-truncation silent failure. Operators triaging an OKX strategy will see 'no daily_pnl' with the only signal being two old `logger.warning('OKX bills fetch failed...')` lines that DO NOT distinguish 'partial' from 'total' failure
summary: The sweep added fail-loud to Bybit (L575-L618) and Binance (L552-L572) but left the OKX branch (L405-L491) untouched. OKX uses an inner pagination loop with `try/except: logger.warning(...); break` at L422-L434 and L451-L469. The break SILENTLY TRUNCATES to whatever bills were fetched before the failure — the result is returned as if complete. The sweep's mention of 'audit-2026-05-07 silent-failure sweep' in the Bybit/Binance comments builds operator expectation that fail-loud has been applied uniformly. For an OKX strategy, the WARNING fires from the paginator but the function returns partial daily_pnl. Caller (analytics_runner) treats partial-data-completed identically to full-data-completed. Strategy gets analytics computed on truncated bills. No DQF flag. silent-failure-hunter MED #2 + #4 flagged this; sweep deliberately scoped them OUT. The chain harm: operators reading the sweep PR title 'silent-failure-sweep — analytics-2026-05-16' assume the entire fetch_daily_pnl function is now fail-loud. It isn't. The mental model is wrong for the third exchange. Adjacent worst-case: a partial truncation on OKX produces dramatically wrong CAGR/Sharpe numbers shown as 'complete' in the factsheet.
evidence: exchange.py:432-434 `except Exception as e: logger.warning('OKX bills fetch failed for %s page %d: %s', ...); break`. exchange.py:467-469 same pattern. exchange.py PR-touched range starts at 552; OKX is intentionally outside scope. silent-failure-hunter explicitly classifies as DEFERRED.
fix: Two viable options for follow-up (this PR doesn't have to fix it, but the PR DESCRIPTION should mention the scoped-out site so operators know the mental model gap). (a) Thread a `partial=True` flag through fetch_daily_pnl's return tuple so the caller can set a DQF for OKX strategies whose paginator truncated. (b) Re-raise at OKX paginator failure and let the outer wrapper at L620 catch it — but then partial data is lost. (a) is the disciplined fix. Minimum: update the PR description with a 'Sites NOT covered: OKX paginator' callout so the next sweep round has a clear target.

title: `outlier ratios failed` WARNING shares the same caplog substring used by the test, but the WARNING name's prefix ('outlier ratios') is also a natural substring that could match an unrelated future log (e.g., 'outlier ratios partially computed' WARNING). The test's tightening to 'outlier ratios failed' is a near-tautology — a refactor that adds a more specific WARNING gets mistakenly counted as the same failure mode
summary: The test at test_metrics.py:1080-1084 filters caplog records by `'outlier ratios failed' in r.getMessage()`. The WARNING is emitted from metrics.py:569-572 with exactly that substring. So today the test passes. But the substring 'outlier ratios' is a future-collision risk: any future enrichment that emits e.g. 'outlier ratios partially computed (1 of 2 succeeded)' OR 'outlier ratios using fallback method due to NaN std' WOULD ALSO match 'outlier ratios' but NOT 'outlier ratios failed'. The test's tightened substring DEFEATS the substring collision risk but ALSO defeats the test's ability to detect a refactor that intentionally renames the failure path. A future refactor that says 'std-based outlier ratios fallback failed' (more accurate) silently breaks the test because the new message doesn't contain 'outlier ratios failed'. This is a coupling-to-string failure mode that round-1's chain MED #7 partially addressed but didn't generalize. CHALLENGE/EXTEND: this is a symptomatic pattern — the test should bind to a STRUCTURED log key, not a substring.
evidence: test_metrics.py:1080 `'outlier ratios failed' in r.getMessage()`. metrics.py:569 emits the exact substring. A refactor to either rename the failure mode or to add a more specific log line would break the test or produce ambiguous matches.
fix: Structural fix: use structlog's structured fields. Change metrics.py:569 to emit a structured log: `logger.warning('outlier ratios failed', extra={'metric_group': 'outlier_ratios', 'metric_names': ['outlier_win_ratio', 'outlier_loss_ratio'], 'returns_len': returns_len_for_log}, exc_info=True)`. Tests then filter on `r.extra.get('metric_group') == 'outlier_ratios'` rather than the human-readable message. Cost: ~5 LoC across metrics.py + test. Benefit: refactors of the user-facing message don't break tests; tests bind to the operational contract (metric group identity).

title: Existing data-quality WARNINGs (NaN-days, catastrophic-returns at L257, L268) share the SAME logger + SAME level as the sweep's 11 new compute-error WARNINGs — operators cannot distinguish 'this strategy's input is unusual' (precondition) from 'qs.stats library failed' (code bug) via Railway log filtering
summary: Pre-sweep, metrics.py:257-272 already emitted WARNINGs for NaN-days and catastrophic-return-days as data-quality preconditions. Those are expected at low frequency for some strategies — they're 'WARN this strategy's input looks unusual'. The sweep adds 11 more WARNINGs at the SAME level on the SAME logger that are 'WARN qs.stats library failed for this scalar' — fundamentally different semantic class (precondition vs runtime error). An operator monitoring 'quantalyze.analytics.metrics WARNING' has no taxonomic separation; the firehose mixes data-quality and code-bug signals. The sweep's stated goal (fail-loud so operators can triage) is undermined: the new WARNINGs sit in the same channel as the existing 'this strategy has some NaN days' signal. Filtering becomes substring-based, which is fragile. Specialist code-reviewer LOW #1/#2 (misnamed prefixes) partly addresses this; the wider issue is missing log taxonomy.
evidence: metrics.py:257 `logger.warning('compute_all_metrics: %d NaN day(s) in returns ...'`. metrics.py:268 `logger.warning('compute_all_metrics: %d return(s) <= -1.0 ...'`. metrics.py:351 `logger.warning('qstats scalar var_1d_95 failed ...'`. All same logger, same level, no structured field distinguishing 'precondition' vs 'compute error'.
fix: Add structured fields. Standardize on `extra={'log_class': 'data_quality' | 'compute_error', 'metric_name': '...', 'returns_len': ...}`. Operators can then alert on `log_class=compute_error` distinctly. Cost: ~15 LoC across metrics.py. Alternative if structlog/structured fields aren't viable: prefix the compute_error messages with a stable tag like 'COMPUTE_ERROR:' so a single grep separates them. The sweep author probably doesn't want to expand scope; minimum mitigation = document in the comment block at L335-L345 that operators should grep for 'qstats scalar' / 'np.percentile' / 'pandas scalar' / 'outlier ratios' / 'benchmark_metrics fan-out' / 'drawdown_episodes computation' as the unified set of compute-error prefixes.
